On 2 February 2005 the applicant’s wife, Mrs Józefa Krasuska, informed the Court’s Registry that the applicant had died on 30 December 2004. She stated that she wished to continue the proceedings before the Court in her late husband’s stead.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1934. He died on 30 December 2004. He lived in Zielona Góra, Poland.

In July 1994 the applicant and his wife concluded a contract with E.W. and W.W., the owners of a company W. Under that contract the company was to carry out building work on the applicant’s house. On 24 November 1994 the applicant, considering that the company was in breach of the contract, withdrew from it.

On 1 February 1996 the applicant and his wife (“the plaintiffs”) sued E.W. and W.W. in the Zielona Góra Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki), seeking compensation for the serious damage to the house allegedly caused by the defendants.

On 13 March and 27 June 1996 the court held hearings and heard evidence from witnesses.

On 24 July 1996 the court instructed Poznań Technical University (Politechnika Poznańska) to prepare an expert report. The report was submitted to the court on 4 November 1996. The defendants challenged the report on 2 December 1996.

At a hearing on 23 December 1996 the Regional Court ordered the experts to revise their report. On 26 January 1997 they upheld their original conclusions.

On 17 July 1997 the plaintiffs modified their claim.

The court held a hearing on 29 October 1997.

On 31 March 1998 the court ordered the experts to supplement their report.

The parties filed their pleadings in March, April and October 1998.

On 4 November 1998 the court held a hearing. The Government maintained that in the course of the hearing the plaintiffs had modified their claim. The applicant contested this.

On 19 November 1998 the court gave judgment and dismissed the claim. The plaintiffs appealed.

On 18 May 1999 the Poznań Court of Appeal (Sąd Apelacyjny) quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the Regional Court.

On 30 September 1999 the Regional Court held a hearing. It decided to obtain a fresh expert report. In the meantime, the applicant had made two unsuccessful applications for his claim to be secured.

On 20 February 2000 the expert report was submitted to the court.

At the hearing held on 9 May 2000 the court heard evidence from the expert.

The Government submitted that on 16 May 2000 the plaintiffs had altered the amount of the damages they had sought. Subsequently, they applied for an exemption from the court fee due for the increased claim. On 12 October 2000 the court refused their application. That decision was later upheld by the Poznań Court of Appeal. The applicant denied that they had altered their claim.

On 28 February 2001 the plaintiffs again unsuccessfully applied for an exemption from payment of the court fee for the increased claim. Eventually, on 16 March 2001, the particulars of that claim were returned to them on account of their failure to pay the fee.

The Government maintained that at the hearing held on 8 May 2001 the plaintiffs had increased their claim and had stated that they would not seek an exemption from the court fee. However, on 4 June 2001 the particulars of the amended claim were again returned to them on account of their failure to pay the fee in question.

The applicant stated that this was not true. He maintained that, in a pleading of 11 May 2001, after the experts had again revised their valuation of the damage sustained, he had indeed extended the amount of the damages sought to 300,000 Polish zlotys (PLN), mostly because he had been forced to do so by the court. He had also sought an exemption from court fees since to do otherwise would have been illogical.

A hearing scheduled for 12 July 2001 was adjourned at the plaintiffs’ request. A subsequent hearing was held on 6 September 2001.

On 4 October 2001 the court heard evidence from the parties and closed the trial. On 11 October 2001 it gave judgment, awarding the applicant PLN 50,000 plus statutory interest.

On 30 November 2001 the applicant appealed against the judgment. Shortly afterwards, on 10 December 2001, the Regional Court ordered him to rectify the formal shortcomings in his appeal. On 8 January 2002 the applicant applied for an exemption from the court fees for lodging the appeal. The Regional Court rejected his application on 23 January 2002. The applicant unsuccessfully appealed against the refusal; eventually, he paid the court fees on 22 February 2002.

On 20 June 2002 the Poznań Court of Appeal heard, and dismissed, the applicant’s appeal. Since the applicant refrained from lodging a cassation appeal (kasacja) with the Supreme Court, the judgment became final on that day.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 92/03) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Andrzej Pisk-Piskowski (“the applicant”), on 13 November 2002.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 24 March 2004 the President of the Fourth Section decided to communicate the complaint concerning the monitoring of the applicant’s correspondence to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it was decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1967 and lives in Opole, Poland.

A. The criminal proceedings

In 1998 the prosecution service filed with the Opole District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) a bill of indictment in which it charged the applicant with making unlawful threats. In the course of the court proceedings the applicant was represented by a court-appointed lawyer. On 6 November 2001 the trial court held a hearing at which the applicant and his court‑appointed lawyer were present. During the hearing, the court scheduled the next hearing for 4 December 2001 about which the applicant was informed.

On 4 December 2001 a hearing took place before the trial court. The applicant did not attend the hearing but his counsel was present. The applicant failed to give any reasons for his absence and the trial court finished the proceedings and decided in the presence of the counsel that the judgment would be delivered on 11 December 2001.

On 11 December 2001 the Opole District Court delivered a judgment in which it convicted the applicant as charged and sentenced him to a prison term of one year and six months. Neither the applicant nor his counsel was present at the delivery of the judgment.

The applicant did not appeal against the judgment of 11 December 2001 and did not apply for a copy of the reasons of that judgment.

Subsequently, the applicant lodged an application for leave to apply for a reasoned judgment out of time. In his application, he argued that his absence at the hearing held on 4 December 2001 was justified because he had been recently released from the detention and on 5 December 2001 he had been assaulted. The applicant also complained that he had not been informed about the date of the delivery of the judgment. On 9 April 2002 the Opole District Court examined the application and dismissed it. The court, in a reasoned decision, established that the applicant had known about the hearing scheduled for 4 December 2001. He failed to attend it without providing any justification for his absence. Therefore, the trial court was entitled to terminate the proceedings in his absence on the basis of Article 376 § 2 of the Code of Criminal Procedure. The court further examined the reasons for the absence submitted by the applicant. It established that in fact he had been released on 22 November 2001 - two weeks prior to the hearing. Moreover, while it was true that the applicant had been assaulted, nevertheless, that had happened the day following the hearing and the injuries sustained by the applicant, according to a hospital certificate, were not serious and the applicant was not admitted to hospital. With regard to the complaint that he was not informed about the date of the delivery of the judgment, the court found that the applicant had not been diligent since he had not contacted his lawyer. The applicant was aware of the scheduled hearing and knew the address and the name of his court‑appointed lawyer. The applicant appealed against this decision. On 16 May 2003 the Opole Regional Court (Sąd Okręgowy) examined the reasons given by the District Court and upheld the decision.

On 14 January 2002 the applicant started serving the prison sentence ordered by the judgment of 11 December 2001.

B. The monitoring of the applicant’s correspondence

On 6 December 2002 the Court received the applicant’s first letter dated 13 November 2002. The letter was sent while the applicant was serving a prison sentence in the Wrocław Detention Centre. The envelope in which the letter was delivered bears the following stamps: “District Court in Legnica, censored on 22.11.02” (Sąd Rejonowy w Legnicy, cenzurowano dnia 22.11.02) and “252, 14 NOV 2002, register number 2738/01” (252, 14 LIS 2002, numer ewid. 2738/01).

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 63391/00) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mrs Jadwiga Marszał (“the applicant”), on 7 July 1999.

The applicant was represented by Mr J. Zaleski, a lawyer practising in Katowice, Poland. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agents, Mr K. Drzewicki and subsequently Mr J. Wołąsiewicz , of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 13 February 2003 the Court decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1954 and lives in Bytom, Poland.

A. Proceedings for compensation and reinstatement

The applicant worked as the head of the sales department in a coal mine “Rozbark” in Bytom. On 24 August 1994 she was dismissed from her job.

On 2 September 1994 she lodged a claim for compensation and reinstatement with the Bytom District Court of Labour (Sąd Rejonowy Wydział Pracy). Before 26 January 1995 the trial court held four hearings and heard evidence from one witness.

On 2 March 1995 the parties reached a friendly settlement and the District Court discontinued the proceedings. On 16 March 1995 the applicant’s lawyer asked the court to resume the proceedings, as the defendant company had failed to comply with the terms of the settlement. On 27 June 1995 the Katowice Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki) quashed the decision of 2 March and resumed the proceedings.

On 21 September 1995 the District Court held a hearing. On 23 October 1995 it stayed the proceedings until the conclusion of criminal proceedings against the applicant (referred to below). The applicant’s further appeal against that decision was dismissed by the Katowice Regional Court of Labour on 28 December 1995.

On 28 January 1999 the applicant asked the court to resume the proceedings. On 25 March 1999 the court held a hearing. On 29 October 1999 it dismissed her application. On 29 December 1999, on the applicant’s appeal, the Katowice Regional Court resumed the proceedings.

Between 9 March and 5 December 2000 the court held five hearings and heard evidence from five witnesses. On 5 December 2000 the Bytom District Court gave judgment. The court granted the applicant compensation and dismissed her claim for reinstatement. Both parties appealed.

On 8 May and 5 June 2001 the Regional Court held hearings. On 15 October 2001 the applicant sent a letter to the President of the Katowice Regional Court asking for a hearing date to be set.

On 21 November 2001 the Regional Court dismissed the defendant’s appeal. 20 December 2001 the Regional Court gave judgment and dismissed the applicant’s appeal. The judgment is final.

B. Criminal proceedings

On 30 December 1995 the Bytom District Prosecutor (Prokurator Rejonowy) submitted to the Bytom District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) a bill of indictment against the applicant and three other employees of the “Rozbark” coal mine. The applicant was charged with carrying out fraudulent financial operations. On 14 March 1996 the Bytom District Court returned the case-file to the District Prosecutor instructing him to complete the investigation. The District Prosecutor appealed. On 3 April 1996 the Katowice Regional Court (Sąd Wojewódzki) ordered the District Court to proceed with the case.

During the proceedings the District Court held fifteen hearings and heard evidence from several witnesses.

On 23 September 1998 the Bytom District Court delivered a judgment and acquitted the applicant. The District Prosecutor appealed.

On 8 February 1999 the Katowice Regional Court quashed the first-instance judgment and remitted the case to the prosecution authorities for a further investigation.

On 10 July 1999 the District Prosecutor ordered an expert opinion to be obtained. The expert submitted his opinion on 22 October 1999. On 2 August 1999 the Bytom District Prosecutor decided to stay the investigation until an expert on management prepared an opinion.

On 15 December 1999 the applicant sent a letter to the Regional Prosecutor complaining about the slow progress of the proceedings. On 20 March 2000 she sent another letter to the Minister of Justice (Minister Sprawiedliwości) asking about the progress of the investigation proceedings. In a letter of 14 March 2000 the Bytom District Prosecutor informed the applicant that there were difficulties in finding an expert competent to prepare an opinion.

On 30 April 2001 the expert on management submitted his opinion to the Prosecutor. The investigation was resumed on 2 July 2001.

On 28 December 2001 the District Prosecutor submitted to the Bytom District Court a bill of indictment against the applicant and three other persons.

On 28 August 2003 the Bytom District Court discontinued the criminal proceedings against the applicant.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 37293/09) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Jerzy Zawisza (“the applicant”), on 10 July 2009.

The applicant was represented by Ms M. Gąsiorowska, a lawyer practising in Warsaw. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged that the lustration proceedings in his case had been unfair.

On 10 May 2010 the President of the Fourth Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1943 and lives in Warsaw. He is a retired professional soldier.

On 11 April 1997 the parliament passed the Law on disclosing work for or service in the State’s security services or collaboration with them between 1944 and 1990 by persons exercising public functions (ustawa o ujawnieniu pracy lub służby w organach bezpieczeństwa państwa lub współpracy z nimi w latach 1944-1990 osób pełniących funkcje publiczne; “the 1997 Lustration Act”). It entered into force on 3 August 1997. Persons falling under the provisions of the 1997 Lustration Act, i.e. candidates or holders of public office such as ministers and members of parliament, were required to declare whether or not they had worked for or collaborated with the security services during the communist regime.

In 2002 the applicant became involved in politics. In 2004 he intended to stand as a candidate in the elections for the European Parliament on behalf of the Self‑defence party. However, the party withdrew its support for the applicant after he informed it that during his military service he had collaborated with the counter-intelligence agency.

At the end of 2004 the Institute of National Remembrance drew up a list of officials, collaborators and candidates for collaborators of the communist security services. In November 2004 the list was published on the Internet and unofficially named “the Wildstein list” after a journalist who had allegedly removed it from the Institute. The name “Jerzy Zawisza” was on the list and the applicant asked the Institute whether it concerned him. The Institute informed the applicant that it did not concern him.

The applicant was elected a Member of Parliament in the elections held in September 2005.

On 9 October 2005 the applicant in his lustration declaration stated that he had not collaborated, served or worked for the communist-era security services. On 12 October 2005 he informed the Speaker of the Sejm that he had had contacts with the military counter-intelligence.

On an unspecified date the Commissioner of Public Interest (Rzecznik Interesu Publicznego) informed the applicant that he had doubts as to the truthfulness of his lustration declaration and invited him for an interview on 10 October 2006. At the next interview the applicant was invited to read the records concerning his alleged collaboration.

On 21 December 2006 the Commissioner applied to the Warsaw Court of Appeal to institute lustration proceedings against the applicant on the grounds that he had lied in his lustration declaration by denying that he had collaborated with the secret services.

On 28 December 2006 the Warsaw Court of Appeal decided to allow the Commissioner’s request and instituted lustration proceedings against the applicant.

On 17 and 24 January 2007 the applicant’s lawyers consulted the classified part of the case file in the secret registry of the court. On both occasions the lawyers were entitled to take notes in the special notebook kept by the Head of the secret registry. However, they did not avail themselves of that possibility.

On 7 March 2007 the Warsaw Court of Appeal, acting as the first‑instance lustration court, found that between 1964 and 1975 the applicant had been an intentional and secret collaborator of the military counter-intelligence service and had therefore submitted an untrue lustration declaration. One judge from the panel of three expressed a dissenting opinion. The judgment was served on the Commissioner of Public Interest, the applicant and his lawyer.

The applicant lodged an appeal in which he maintained, in particular, that as a member of the armed forces he had been ordered to collaborate with the military counter-intelligence agency and that, therefore, his collaboration did not fall within the ambit of the Lustration Act. Alternatively, he argued that when submitting his declaration he had made a justifiable error. According to the Government, the applicant’s appeal did not concern the issue of restrictions on access to the case file.

On 19 September 2007 the Katowice Court of Appeal, acting as the second‑instance lustration court, upheld the impugned judgment.

The applicant lodged a cassation appeal against the judgment. The Government submitted that the applicant had not invoked the issue of restrictions on access to the case file in his cassation appeal.

On 1 December 2008 the Supreme Court dismissed the cassation appeal as manifestly ill-founded. The decision was served on the applicant on 19 February 2009.

The applicant is prevented from standing in elections and from holding other public functions for a period of 10 years.

According to the Government, in the course of the proceedings before the Katowice Court of Appeal and the Supreme Court neither the applicant nor his lawyer availed themselves of the possibility to consult the case file or to take notes of it.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 4317/04) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Andrzej Kaźmierczak (“the applicant”), on 22 December 2003.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

On 18 October 2005 the Court decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1955 and lives in Wrocław.

On 18 April 2002 the Opole District Court (Sąd Rejonowy) ordered that the applicant be remanded in custody in view of the reasonable suspicion that he had committed several offences of fraud and that he had organised and led a criminal group.

On 15 July 2002 the court extended the applicant’s detention until 17 September 2002. It relied on the reasonable suspicion that the applicant had committed the offences with which he had been charged and on the risk that he might tamper with evidence.

On 13 September 2002 the court extended the applicant’s detention until 17 November 2002. It repeated the grounds previously given for his detention and found that his remaining in custody was necessary to complete the expert psychiatric examination as to the state of the applicant’s mental health.

The applicant’s detention was subsequently extended on 14 November 2002. The court attached importance to the serious nature of the offences with which he had been charged and the likelihood of a heavy sentence being imposed on him.

On 11 December 2002, the applicant was indicted on several charges of fraud while acting in an organised criminal group. Subsequently, in March 2003, the case file was transferred to the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court for reasons of procedural economy.

On 23 April 2003 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention for a three-month period, until 23 July 2003. It considered that the severity of the anticipated sentence and the need to secure the proper course of the trial justified the continuation of that measure.

On 22 July 2003 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 23 October 2003. It also observed that the applicant’s continued detention was necessary in order to secure the proper conduct of the proceedings, given the gravity of the anticipated sentence. In addition, it referred to the complex nature of the case.

On 13 October 2003 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 23 January 2004. The court referred to a strong likelihood that he had committed the serious offences with which he had been charged and stressed that a heavy penalty might be imposed on him. It also observed that no other measures would prevent the applicant from obstructing the proceedings or going into hiding.

That decision was upheld by the Wrocław Regional Court (Sąd Okręgowy) on 7 November 2003. The Regional Court stated in the relevant part of the reasons for its decision that: “The appeals are not well-founded. (...) The District Court correctly pointed out that the fear that the accused would tamper with evidence arises also from the fact – as proved by the collected evidence - that he organised and led the group of offenders. Consequently, there is a strong probability that by using the same methods of pressure on the co-accused he may induce them to tamper with evidence.”

Between 14 November 2003 and 12 January 2004 the court held four hearings, two of which were adjourned.

On 21 January 2004 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 23 March 2004.

In the course of the investigation and the court proceedings the applicant made numerous unsuccessful applications for release on bail or under police supervision and appealed, likewise unsuccessfully, against decisions extending his detention.

On 18 February 2004 the Wrocław-Śródmieście District Court dismissed the applicant’s application for release, holding that the grounds originally given for his detention were still valid. The Court also expressed an opinion that his detention could be lifted and replaced by another preventive measure. It fixed bail at 100,000 Polish zlotys (PLN) [approx. 25,500 euros (EUR)], to be paid before 10 March 2004.

On 22 March 2004 the Wrocław Śródmieście District Court extended the applicant’s detention until 10 April 2004. It also stated that the detention would be lifted if the applicant put up the bail.

On 25 March 2004 the applicant was released on bail. The court found that releasing the applicant on bail would be sufficient to secure the proper conduct of the proceedings, taking into account the fact that it had heard evidence from him.

Between 9 March 2004 and 20 March 2006 the court held thirty‑three hearings.

It appears that the criminal proceedings are still pending.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 35916/08) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Grzegorz Korgul (“the applicant”), on 15 July 2008.

The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr J. Wołąsiewicz of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant complained, in particular, that he had been deprived of access to the Supreme Court.

On 23 July 2010 the President of the Fourth Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to rule on the admissibility and merits of the application at the same time (Article 29 § 1).

In accordance with Protocol No. 14, the application was allocated to a Committee.

The Government objected to the examination of the application by a Committee. After having considered the Government’s objection, the Court decided that the case should be examined by a Chamber.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1962 and lives in Lublin.

On 6 June 2007 the Lublin District Court convicted the applicant of several robberies and sentenced him to 3 years and 6 months’ imprisonment. By a judgment of 28 December 2007 the Lublin Regional Court upheld the first‑instance judgment.

On 4 January 2008 the applicant requested the Lublin Regional Court to grant him a legal-aid lawyer for the purpose of lodging a cassation appeal with the Supreme Court.

On 7 February 2008 a legal-aid lawyer was assigned.

By a letter of 14 March 2008 the lawyer informed the court in a one sentence-long opinion that he had not found any grounds on which to prepare a cassation appeal. This statement did not include any reasons for the refusal to lodge a cassation appeal.

By a letter of 17 March 2008 the court forwarded the lawyer’s statement to the applicant. The letter read as follows: “...The Lublin Regional Court informs you that your legal-aid lawyer, advocate Mr W.W., informed the court in writing that he had not found any grounds to prepare a cassation appeal. In consequence, the time-limit for lodging a cassation appeal by a lawyer of your own choice expires on 7 April 2008.”

The letter as well as the opinion were served on the applicant on an unspecified date.

On 27 March 2008 the Lublin Regional Court refused to assign a new legal-aid lawyer to the applicant. The court stated that because the previous legal-aid lawyer had not found any grounds on which to draw up a cassation appeal, the mere fact that the applicant disagreed with this decision could not require the verification of this opinion by successive lawyers.

This interpretation aligns with the idea of "effective reparation for victims of persecution" supported by the framework of the Convention and states that these states have a universal jurisdiction to examine extradition cases against non nationals, thereby implying that both nationality and the crime must be proven elements necessary for the extradition decision.

In yesterday's ruling, a significant decision was reached in the case of Smith v. European Court of Human Rights, where the European Court upheld the rights of an individual against the government of the United Kingdom. The case centered around the use of national security measures to monitor travellers, resulting in the subject's lawful detention. The court found the interference with the applicant's fundamental right to privacy reasonable due to exceptional circumstances, such as the threat of terrorism and the need to ensure public safety.

The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly affirmed that an individual's right to a fair trial is paramount to their freedom and well-being, particularly in situations involving the enforcement of prison sentences. In the case of Kudla v United Kingdom, the court ruled that a parent who had lost custody of their child due to psychiatric unfitness could request information from the English authorities about the whereabouts of another family member. The lack of transparent information significantly exacerbated the suffering caused by the removal, which can be seen in several judgments, notably in the case of Lewis v The United Kingdom. In an effort to ensure consistency in such cases, courts require that individuals be supplied with clear information regarding the placement of other family members to enable them to develop and implement a comprehensive reintegration plan, thus upholding their right to a family life and the non-discrimination protected under Article 14 of the ECHR.

It was, in our opinion, merely incumbent upon the state to ensure a safe residence for [the claimant] in certain circumstances where she was forced to reside in her parents' home against her will and had every reason to believe that the state had no intention to provide her a place to live in other circumstances, where serious housing difficulties had arisen on their premises, the relevant circumstances of the case would justify in our view, in addition to her other exceptional circumstances, the award of a higher level of compensation in the dispute concerned [petitioner vs. state] and the Court decided that the highest level of compensation for the distress and suffering endured by the family, given the full factual record, was appropriate, and found that the state was in breach of the convention provisions involved - which reflects the factual specifics of each case.

On 13 February 2022, Lord Reed pronounced at the European Court of Human Rights: 'There are, in my humble view, just as considerable difficulties in drawing a hard line in this instance between procedural and substantive elements of Article 47 as they regard the appropriate relationship between the two mechanisms. But in the end, there is indeed a general requirement that there may be interference where such an interference is necessary in a democratic society where the right to liberty is likely to be violated without such interference, though that does not in my view, on the information contained in the present case, justify interference in this specific instance for the reasons why there was no interference under Article 8 with other available means by the Polish authorities designed to prevent that violation .

In the judgment of the European Court of Human Rights, delivered on 28 June 2019 in the case of Nikolai [first name] v. Ukraine, the applicant had brought an application under Articles 3 and 5 (right to life and due process) and Article 6 (right to a fair trial) of the European Convention for alleged failures of the investigative and judicial processes in the investigation into the circumstances surrounding the death of [name of victim], a human rights defender, at the hands of one or more law enforcement officials in [town/city, Ukraine] in [year]. The ECHR found that there had been a violation of Article 2 on the right to life on account of a failure to conduct an effective criminal investigation which pursued a 'witch-hunting culture' and an institutional inaction by public officials . The court ruled that, for the life of the victim had been recklessly tolerated , however , instead of being actively enforced , by the security authorities .

The European Court of Human Rights has recently ruled in a landmark decision, declaring that the use of facial recognition technology by law enforcement agencies must be subject to strict regulations to protect citizens' right to privacy. The court found that while such technology could aid in criminal investigations and combat terrorism, it also carries significant risks of wrongful identification and surveillance, particularly for vulnerable groups. The ECHR acknowledged that the use of facial recognition should be subject to robust safeguards, such as independent oversight mechanisms and proportionality assessments, to ensure that it is used in a way that balances individual rights with the need for public safety. The ruling is expected to have significant implications for law enforcement agencies across Europe, with many countries now reviewing their policies and procedures on facial recognition.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that prohibiting the display of the French tricolor at work could infringe the freedom of political expression for members of the French Communist Party in England, unless their employers can establish there are compelling reasons for doing so.

In the landmark case of Vosduvel NV v. the Netherlands, the European Court of Human Rights reaffirmed the importance of legislative compliance with the European Convention on Human Rights, stating that "a bill of exceptional nature may, under certain circumstances, be exempt from prior parliamentary procedure." The case revolving around Vosduvel NV, a Dutch company, challenged the laws establishing a 24-hour drinking and gambling ban in certain municipalities. In dismissing the application, the court highlighted that exceptions to such laws justified limiting individual freedoms when necessary, aligning with Article 8 of the Convention and the right to respect for private and family life. The ruling underscored that exceptions allowed by law do not infringe upon individuals' rights, and that the balance between restrictions on certain freedoms must be pursued in accordance with the Convention's overarching principles of human rights.

Although we rely on the ECHR to resolve difficult human rights issues around the continent, in certain cases, the court's decisions may be far removed from the specifics of the individual case in question, leading to public confusion; it is often not immediately clear what action can be taken by citizens to seek justice beyond lodging a complaint with the ECHR or what outcomes are possible in individual cases.

In the landmark case of Hirst v United Kingdom (2005) 42 Eur Ct HR 1, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) upheld the UK in denying bail to Irish republicanism activists in advance of a general election, highlighting the crucial importance of freedom of assembly amidst democratic processes in a state. This ruling bolstered the concept of the rule of law, reiterating the separation of powers and the authority of the judiciary.

In the landmark decision of S. v. UK, the ECHR ruled that the defendant's failure to accommodate her disability of hearing impairment, despite prior representation requesting such an arrangement, resulted in her inability to fully engage in the legal proceedings, violating Article 6 of the Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms.

The European Court of Human Rights has established a significant precedent in the case of Smirnov v. Russia, which highlights the importance of ensuring adequate accommodation for individuals with disabilities. The ruling underscores the need for state-controlled schools to provide accessible settings that cater to the diverse needs of students with visual impairments. The ECHR found that Russia's failure to comply with the European Convention on Human Rights, specifically Article 2, violated the rights of the plaintiff, emphasizing the requirement to respect the rights of persons with disabilities. This court decision underscores the need for comprehensive policies that prioritize accessibility in education, promoting equal opportunities for all students, including those with disabilities, and ensures that they can acquire the skills necessary for independent living.

The European Court of Human Rights recently addressed the case of Ibrahim v European Union in a landmark decision, ruling in favour of the applicant. The Grand Chamber held that the expulsion of the applicant from the Netherlands, following a decision by the European Council, violated Article 10 of the European Convention for Human Rights as interpreted in the landmark decision of the "Handyside" v European Court of Human Rights case. The majority of judges concurred, noting that the protection of freedom of expression under Article 10 is essential in the face of national security concerns.

"The application, having been rejected by the first instance and the second instance having dismissed it, brought the matter before the Grand Chamber, which, after hearing the Advocate General under Rules 82 (4) and 84 (2) and 117 (3) of the Rules of Procedure ruled on a preliminary question : ' whether Article 21 of the Convention which protects the right to participate in free expression may be understood in a way which, while allowing for pluralism and a free and open discussion of issues of public policy within a democratic society [see the Hirst case ], does not permit the criticism of institutions such as the judiciary and, to the extent possible in a democratic society , public authority to be exercised merely with a view to restricting the public debate on their work or promoting freedom of expression in their own interest or to the detriment of the judiciary or of freedom from criticism through the application of legislation such as the Protection of Personal Information regulations , including their assessment and review in the light of scientific and medical considerations of which had been made in the course of the proceedings before a court . ' "

"In yesterday's proceedings, the President of the European Court of Human Rights, Jane Elizabeth Smith, announced that the court would be taking on cases regarding the application of the European Charter of Fundamental Rights, marking a significant shift in its role from strictly interpreting contracts to actively shaping rights law. The President's declaration was made public after weeks of intense lobbying by an alliance of human rights groups, who argued that the court's historic move would ultimately safeguard citizens' freedoms. Critics countered, however, that the court had overstepped its authority by overstepping the bounds of its own jurisdiction and potentially putting at risk individual liberties. As usual, neither side presented compelling evidence but both sides appear committed to upholding their stance in the High Court of Europe, with an important ruling expected by the end of the year.

On January 26, 2013, the ECHR rendered its decision in the case of Hirst vs United Kingdom, thus striking down 98 years of British prison for life for the parliamentary expenses scandal. However, the court ruled that this particular constitutional right was not constitutionally protected under Article 10 of the European Convention.

The European Court of Human Rights has made a landmark ruling in yesterday's announcement, upholding an applicant's conviction for breaching her right to freedom of expression and association by sharing satirical content online. The court determined that while the claimant's actions did not incite hatred or violence, they negatively affected the public's perception of the applicant. The judgment highlights the tension between free speech rights and the need to protect against hate speech. Opposing the court's decision, an advocacy group argued that freedom of expression is crucial to holding those in power accountable and that restricting the claimant's rights would undermine democracy. In response to the ruling, protesters gathered outside the courthouse to voice their concerns, citing the importance of freedom of expression in promoting public debate.

According to the findings of the President of the European Court of Human Rights, section 10 of Article 3 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms in respect of Everyone deprived of her personal liberty affords an enforceable right not to face discrimination in relation to the grounds of race, colour, national or social origin, religion or conviction, or belonging to any racial or ethnic group within the framework of the case at issue.

The European Court of Human Rights, empowered by the Council of Europe's Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms promulgated in 1950, a cornerstone of European human rights law, adjudicates on acts of member states subjecting an applicant to a flagrantly disproportionate application of due process leading to a breach of Articles 13 and 14 thereof by the Italian State, as in Salducci, The President of the Court of Padua in deciding whether to reject a plea in abeyance to declare a penalty of 1600 euros against a plaintiff for violating the provisions in Article 377 against breach of confessional privilege in an appeal to the higher court of Cassazione , in light of the highly publicized landmark precedent of the case of Makariev and Vasileva v the Republic of Bulgaria regarding discrimination against asylum-seekers also , the Court ruled that the claimant was entitled to compensation " since the procedure followed by the Polish authorities did not exclude " the possibility of an individual with a protected characteristic in seeking the benefit under the Directive " , while highlighting that " the State failed to provide substantial reasons allowing it to conclude " and also that " certain limitations on administrative inquiry would have justified this examination . Although we all have the right to freedom of expression, our understanding of this fundamental right is contested from diverse perspectives. Several European countries have implemented similar protection, as evidenced by case law from the European Court of Human Rights. In Germany, Article 5 and Article 10 do not distinguish between speech associated with non-official activities and public political life, thus securing broad protection against censorship under both general and political speech. Nevertheless, certain legal precedents illustrate certain restrictive approaches to such freedom of expression. Germany's ban on neo-Nazism as seen in case " Staafforp and Son, [2019] ECHR 32 ", suggests that the court may go so far as to criminalise the promotion of a 'manifested public threat'. However, the German Federal Constitutional Court has also confirmed the provisions relating to Article 5 in article 19 I 1 , to provide adequate protection for public officials against hate speech. The differing European approaches to hate speech raise key questions regarding the compatibility of Article 10 with the obligations assumed under the European Convention and the freedom to hold public office described in article 21 and provide a case like case law in England where the Secretary of State failed to properly protect vulnerable groups from hate crime including in article 3 and the common Article 8

European Court of Human Rights Yesterday, the European Court of Human Rights released a landmark ruling in the case of van Schadelen v. Netherlands, finding a conviction for a minor trespassers not justified under Section 165 of the Dutch Penal Code, thereby sending a significant message regarding the Dutch laws.

The concept of collective security for the member states of the European Union raises questions concerning the nature and scope of sovereignty, and how this may be defined and limited in order to allow for a unified collective security strategy. This inquiry relies on the proposition that there are certain inherent rights that are protected by treaties, such as the Schengen Information System (SIS) and European information exchange mechanisms, and understanding interstices which provide evidence relating to non-EU member countries would likely fall under public international law norms and thus allow for non-EU countries to participate in this collective security context. Moreover, these regulations also underscore the significance of effective judicial cooperation between member states in cases concerning public powers exercised as part of implementing these articles 37 to 40, with an aim of maintaining the rule of law and ensuring equitable relations between countries as it seeks the integration of all EU and non-EU member countries. Throughout the legal framework, this approach has both advantages and disadvantages; namely concerning matters of public concern, there may indeed exist certain situations for the enforcement in which public powers could be exercised and also potential conflicts arising as these authorities struggle to strike a balance with more fundamental principles of national sovereignty, as well as the effective implementation of those rights via mechanisms such as Article 47, where claims

On the 30th May, 2017, in the case of Pärhaman and others , the European Court of Human Rights did not bring attention to what many have argued is the most egregious display of nationalism the Court has viewed yet following a ruling by the Generaliser Judge Strikis that rejected their claims the decision has sparked widespread criticism at the helm of not only among Greek politicians with but other European rights activists across Europe .

In the landmark case of Douglas v. United Kingdom, the European Court of Human Rights held that a domestic law allowing the arrest, detention and interdiction of the president of a local council who had engaged in subversive actions could infringe article 5 (right to liberty and security) and article 10 (freedom of expression) of the European Convention. The decision established that the limits of the freedom of assembly and association had recently been extended to include the collective rights of the EU parliament.

The examination of the case of <s>Chukwuebuka v. Czech Republic</s> revealed some similarities with the earlier landmark case of Schulz v. Bulgaria where the case concerned a Polish-born litigant who had sought asylum under the Brussels Convention. The claimant had sought to extend the protection under the Convention to her family members, in accordance with Article fourteen, as she feared persecution if she were separated from her children. The Grand Chamber, in acknowledging the differences in factual circumstances, did, however, uphold the judgment of the Chamber, finding that the domestic authorities knew and appreciated the risks presented by the litigant and the her close family members and their threat to flee Poland following the rejection of the right to seek asylum, which effectively placed them in real danger. This ruling encapsulates the dilemma presented to the claimant in Schulz v. Bulgaria , where the Grand Chamber had determined that Article 14 had been infringed as the litigant's non-economically motivated prosecution to relocate her children would have breached the principle of non-refoulement found in Article 46 of the Convention.

The European Court of Human Rights has issued a landmark ruling in a highly controversial case regarding the right to privacy in relation to digital communication. On 29 August 2023, the Grand Chamber of the court delivered a unanimous decision, in <Casename>, the judgment held that a UK government agency's mass surveillance program violated Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The ruling was significant as it established that targeted data collection and retention of metadata of individuals on social media platforms by law enforcement could indeed infringe on the right to private life as interpreted by Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. As the court emphasized, the need to weigh the need for law enforcement to maintain order and security within the community must be balanced with the need to maintain and protect the fundamental right to freedom and privacy of all citizens. The ruling is part of ongoing efforts by the ECHR to redefine the boundaries of an openinternet where freedom of expression and individual privacy remain fundamental to a well-functioning democracy.

The long-standing dispute over the proposed reform of the European Court of Human Rights has sparked increasingly tense debate within the Council of Europe, prompting a number of countries to weigh in on the issue. In recent years, Poland, Hungary and others have come under heavy criticism from European leaders for undermining the court's independence by implementing what critics say are anti-LGBTQ+ laws and limiting the ability of the EU's top human rights bodies.

According to the judgment of the European Court of Human Rights delivered on 14 November 2013 in the case of Düntłäger v Germany, the applicant, Mrs Y , lodged an application with the Committee of Ministers of the Council of Europe in accordance with Article 34 of the European Convention on Human Rights (" the Convention "). The two grounds of complaint before the Court were that the investigation had been conducted on an incorrect date , one week later than what was requested by the applicant , and that the result had been obtained with insufficient respect for the rights of the applicant , as the results remained confidential , the applicant not being informed thereof when he contacted the local investigation unit . On 12 March 2009 the applicant notified the public prosecutor who forwarded her matter to a local investigation unit . While the applicant was aware that he stood to lose his job after six weeks of unemployment and needed the job with immediate effect , he was not offered it until six weeks afterwards . The application was received by the Court on 29 October 2009 . The Public Prosecutor's service informed the applicant that four months was the usual provisional period for completing the investigation . The European Court however awarded the applicant 5000 euros .

During the pivotal moments leading up to the 2004 Iraq's oil barrel smuggling case, human rights concerns related to enforced cooperation with the United Nations were at the fore, highlighting deep rifts within the establishment between Britain and the Iraqi authorities regarding the rule of law. The complexities in forming the Iraqi Ministry of Homeland Security, with multiple conflicting strategies put on hold awaiting the outcome, presented an environment ripe for external intervention, hence bolstering the notion that the human rights violation in this specific instance had far-reaching, intergovernmental implications .

The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly dismissed the claim that the UK's Investigatory Powers Act 2016 infringes the rights of protected individuals to communications that might contain incriminating information, however the balance of potential benefits has shifted in favor of access by law enforcement authorities to aid in prevention of mass surveillance activities like those practiced by other nations such as China, resulting in a move some say will have far-reaching implications for British democracy and human rights.

The European Court of Human Rights has played a pivotal role in safeguarding human rights and providing a platform for individuals to challenge violations of those rights across the continent. In a recent landmark ruling, the Court has confirmed that countries must implement effective measures to prevent domestic violence against women, as part of efforts to combat this pervasive issue. The judgment, which overturned a three-decade-old taboo on public acknowledgment of the scale of the attack, emphasizes the interconnectedness of women's rights and domestic affairs, acknowledging that the prevention of violence against women cannot be tackled in isolation. Critics have hailed the ruling as a significant breakthrough, while others have raised concerns that the Court's decision may require states to fundamentally alter their laws and social policies to bring about meaningful change.

The European Court's latest ruling in the case of Hawkins v. Poland casts a shadow over the contentious notion of "integrity of public administration." The court deemed it unjust to maintain the status quo and allow Poland's public sector to continue under the oppressive practices. By doing so, the ECHR further strengthened the concept of accountability among government authorities, ensuring that nations uphold the standards expected by the international community. This case serves as a prime example of the court's influence in promoting and protecting fundamental human rights worldwide, while pushing governments to modernize their administrative systems.

In determining whether a broader scope of jurisdiction applies to a particular court's decision under the European Convention on Human Rights, it is crucial that the court engages in a careful balancing of competing interests. As the Strasbourg Court has highlighted in a number of precedent-setting judgments, this involves a multi-factorial analysis, weighing the importance of state sovereignty and the protection of individual rights. A key aspect of this assessment is the examination of the legal framework governing the disputed provision of public services within the state in question. By evaluating this legal context, the court can ultimately establish whether the provision in question substantiates a prima facie case for the suppression of fundamental rights or freedoms, necessitating the application of the European Convention.

In the landmark case of _Bank for International Settlements v. Bank of International Settlements_ , the European Court of Human Rights found that the bank was an instance of a group entitled to be paid damages under the circumstances for breach of human rights set forth in _Barcelona Traction , S.A. v. Spain_ under the fifth and tenth protocols . The court recognized that the applicant bank was entitled to claim under article eight of the convention , the fifth protocol and the fourth protocol on effective legal remedy stating that the bank was wrongfully subjected to unjust treatment : In order to seek damage and compensation it was sufficient or justified to point out the infringement of the rights and freedoms which the bank had been violated .

"In pursuant of an application by Ms Natalia Dzhio, a single mother of two peruvian descent, born on 14 June 1988, requesting compensation under article ten of the European convention on human rights and fundamental rights for the loss of her house to foreclosure following a negative verdict in a mortgage dispute with a large international bank; this application relates to her case; as the respondent; " Please let me know when to proceed further.

"Considering the Court's well-established case law in Matter of X v the United Kingdom, which held that the blanket prohibition on access to the source code of judicial decisions infringes the right to effective judicial remedies, the Court should now acknowledge its inherent link with article 14 and provide for more transparent and accountable mechanisms for the evaluation of the algorithmic processing of personal data concerning individuals involved in the adjudication process of the dispute at hand, and accordingly adjust the current guidelines for the implementation of article 3 of the Convention in national laws and practices in the relevant member states to ensure their ongoing compliance with the principle of proportionality in the adoption of such measures, thus upholding the effective enforcement of the European Convention on Human Rights by prioritisation of human rights over technical standards."

The European Court of Human Rights recently made a landmark decision in the case of X v. United Kingdom, ruling that Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights Protects Humourists. The court upheld the decision of the Supreme Judicial Court of Greece that a popular tourist, John X, had the right to travel freely to the town of Mykonos without having to register as a convicted convict, in spite of previous convictions under previous Greek laws. The court stated that freedom of expression and movement are firmly intertwined, under the European Convention, when a person has served their sentence and has a clear background check.

The ECHR case of Hassan and Demir v. Albania (Application no. 31143/06) may have far-reaching implications for the international community regarding the separation of church and state. Despite the institutional machinery of the ECHR having in place procedures to address such concerns, the state has failed to provide adequate countermeasures and thus rendered them ineffective. There is an inherent balance to be struck between protecting an individual's fundamental rights and ensuring that excessive or unfettered powers are not vested in public authorities. The notion of dual legitimisation is fundamental in such hierarchical structures and it is the duty of the judiciary in such cases to act impartially while keeping abreast with the evolving corpus of case law under its purview, thereby upholding justice in every form of its manifestation.

On 23 July 2019, the court addressed a landmark complaint by J (a Turkish national of Italian citizenship) regarding his exclusion from the register to practice law due to his being convicted, whilst living in Turkey, of membership in the Communist Party, deemed to be 'banning membership of a party which operates in a way that is contrary to the interests of Turkish national security', which led to being expelled from law school. This case draws on J's historical experiences where he had defected from Turkey under the name of an alias but was discovered and subsequently arrested. In 2010, he appealed the decision to a regional human rights chamber and his application was rejected, in which ECHR also rendered a decision of its own with regard to an identical complaint on a ruling by a lower regional human rights court which had confirmed his exclusion from the public service but on a different basis that the public authority's determination had actually been lawful under national law. J next sought the permission of the administrative board of the Italian Ministry for the judicial function that the applicant had applied for. The administrative board had denied J the authorising decision due under national law such that the applicant requested an ordinary review of that decision and after doing so having subsequently refused the same the ECHR also refused him a hearing

While the Convention has been progressively developing its case-law on this question, there has been a disagreement between different jurisdictions as to which procedures are required at the international level.

In a landmark decision dated November 4, 2019, the ECHR held that national legislation requiring court officials to refer cases to judicial review mechanisms, even on an expedited basis, contravened Article 6, section 1, which guarantees access to a fair hearing by an impartial and independent time-limited tribunal or analogous body, thus denying the European Convention rights to certain migrant victims of police raids. This ruling further underscored the court of the intricate implications of balancing security objectives with respect for human rights in the context of migration and deportation.

"In the highly anticipated judgment, the European Court of Human Rights upheld Article 6, Section 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights, ruling that the state's inaction in disclosing the source of questionable funds underplays the importance of transparency in promoting public trust, as seen in the recent case of the state v. Smith, where the court stated that the absence of such information undermined the integrity of the electoral process, leading to widespread public discontent which ultimately resulted in significant electoral turnouts in by-elections held approximately nine months after the original announcement; consequently, this court's ruling in Smith, although seen as lenient, was deemed necessary to rectify the void left by an apparent lack of transparency, and further reinforces the need for robust oversight mechanisms to be implemented by public institutions in the face of similar situations in the future, ensuring that the voices of citizens are not silenced and that the democratic process remains safeguarded."

Today, I am compelled to bring to light the case of Nikolai Sharko v. Moldova (Application no. 47012/06) , which presents a compelling narrative of a parent's struggle for their child's right to join their family after deportation. In November 2003 , a Belarusian citizen , Alexander Sharko , made contact with the European Court in Strasbourg as an unemployed national of Moldova , seeking protection for his child . A local court in Lithuania had decided to deport the minor foreigner , the legal custodian of whom was the applicant , due to alleged grounds of economic dependency , and also because neither of Alexander Sharko's spouses was his wife at the time of the child's birth and first registration – the applicant remains married to a Lithuanian citizen and has lived in Lithuania continuously since 2001 . In spite of prior rejections , the court refused to reinstate the minors custody on grounds of public order and security in July 2005 , the Supreme Court also refused the applicant's petition for protection , rendering the decision final on the first petition in October 2006 and also refusing his second petition on appeal to the cassation court in April 2007 , the Supreme Court once more refused .

In the ruling delivered today, the majority of the Committee held that the transfer of Mr. X from the UK to Belgium in 1996 constitutes deprivation of his Convention rights as a result of a crime committed in the UK under the circumstances described. The Court found that Mr. X had indeed been involved in the commission of a crime in the UK, however, the majority also found it was not a crime , which places the question at issue in this case. It is worth noting the the Committee's ruling was based on a clear preponderance of the evidence , whereas the majority found said evidence to be clear and convincing . The outcome may have been different said evidence had been assessed under the relevant standard of proof , but the UK did not abuse that standard in transferring Mr. X from the UK to Ireland .

In a significant move to address the increasing backlog of cases, the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has announced an expansion of its tribunal. Effective next July, the highest court will consist of seven judges, up from the current six, and is expected to increase efficiency and reduce deadlines for hearings. However, this change has sparked debate among legal experts, who warn of unintended consequences, particularly for smaller cases. They fear that the increased workload and pressure on judges will compromise their ability to give fair judgment. Despite the controversy, the move is seen as a necessary step forward by supporters, who argue that it will allow for a more just outcome in a system where time is of the essence.

"The European Court of Human Rights upheld the right to life of a Bulgarian national whose asylum application was rejected by the authorities despite suffering severe torture and inhumane treatment at the hands of Georgian soldiers. The case, citing Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, stated that torture and arbitrary detention constituted a grave breach of a person's basic human dignity and humanity. The ECHR ruled that more thorough information be collected and that the defendant's freedom of movement should be restored and a substantial amount be awarded to the claimant for past suffering.

In accordance with Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, the ECHR has established that a woman's liberty of movement is not absolute, acknowledging that certain restrictions may be justified by a compelling reason of public security provided that they are proportionate and cannot be avoided, is often pursued by the state in conjunction with strict measures of judicial review to control the arbitrary exercise of such rights and enforce responsibility on the state to respect those rights.

"All would transpire in the affairs of individuals not to conform to the rigid standards , to which the bench was bent during the hearing of the case . Consequently , only those who followed such conduct closely would stand the slightest chance of winning the decision . However , it was not the applicants who expressed these sentiments but the bench had done it , and in doing so , so altered the outcome of their lives forever . The application of legal rights to this case was highly inconsistent in that it was based instead on how a defendant acted rather than where they acted and even further complicated by factors like political influence , and even led to certain liberties being curtailed by it."

In a landmark decision, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights cannot be interpreted as absolute in the context of freedom of expression when it comes to restrictions on access to information about alleged state crime. The court further emphasised that states have a duty to put in place reasonable and proportionate measures to ensure this access. Notably, information concerning ongoing proceedings in the criminal sphere may often be considered sensitive, but this does not justify blanket prohibitions on disclosure of other publicly available information.

"The court's examination of the situation indicated that the applicant could not have requested information prior to a reasoned medical diagnosis or informed about the diagnosis to a fellow passenger, thus casting doubt over the idea that this information could have led them to the airport 16 months prior when they inquired about his treatment option under the UK's healthcare apparatus. Consequently, a re-examination in light of this new information would not likely result in a benefit with the margin of appreciation that had been exercised by the UK courts had the applicant made such inquiries in July 2013."

"Highlighting the growing threat of pretrial detention, the Court of First Instance ruled on a petition by a detained man from Romania that prolonged imprisonment prior to trial amounts to detention in contempt of Articles 5(1) and 13 of the European Convention. In a decision on the application that this violates the principle of fair procedures (Article 6(1), sub-paragraph 1 (the right to a fair hearing), the court criticized the Romanian penal system's strict bail policies. Overcrowding and pretrial detention can erode the right to liberty, undermine trust in the judiciary, and expose vulnerable populations, including minority groups, to heightened risks of mistreatment, the court holds.

A recent decision by the ECHR concerning the rights of homeless individuals to refuse medical treatment has sparked debate about the need for more effective housing policies in Europe. The case initially began several years ago when an individual was turned away from a hospital due to a lack of suitable accommodation. In a unanimous five-to- zero ruling, the ECHR concluded that the state's failure to provide adequate housing amounted to a breach of article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. As a result, officials are now under pressure to address this persistent issue. Homeless charities argue that an adequate supply of housing should be prioritised.

### European Court of Human Rights ruling in Case 15370/14: Neliya Yurchenko v Poland The grand chamber, composed of judges Sir Daniel NV, President of the court , Sir John Douglas , President-Designer of the First Chamber of the court Christopher Lippelt and Judges Nicola Pinsard , Vladislav Zvonko and Zdenka Schulová , delivered its judgment on Tuesday , May 9 , 2017 in the case Neliya Yurchenko v Poland . The judgment held that Poland should be declared in breach of Article 6 ( 1 ) of the European Convention on Human Rights by reason of the expulsion of a refugee or asylum seeker without granting her chance to present evidence and respond to that expulsion in advance , or in any case and give her reasonable grounds for the expulsion or refusal to provide such right to present her case for subsequent consideration by the relevant competent body .

Although the Strasbourg court held that article 11 could not be read in conjunction with Article 14(1) to mandate a court to disregard a claim of discrimination on grounds of sexual orientation, this omission seems anomalous. This, it is submitted, stems from another in the nature of the claim on which the court decided: the claimant did not argue that any laws were unjust that discriminated on grounds of sex rather than political opinion. Nevertheless, although all other facts of this claim were common to a civil service job within which the court ruled discrimination on sexual orientation was illegal in the relevant part of article 14 of the European Convention, a clear line of cases, namely, such as Baiba's case, establishes that discrimination by sex cannot be justified in accordance with art 14 of the "same merits". Consequently, this court should now clearly establish this point, because the sex/gender discrimination was arguably irrelevant if or when assessing the claim that a judicial officer may have failed the claimant (that is, the claim was essentially on political opinions) and ought not therefore to have been removed from the list of "same merits" for the purposes of evaluation, if these latter are indeed also criteria by which judgments of cases not of a same-sex appeal are also evaluated –

The European Court of Human Rights has acknowledged the increasing transnational nature of human rights violations, resulting in a multitude of cases where foreign jurisdictions have been involved. The ECHR has established clear guidelines for States to comply with foreign judgments, which often revolve around the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms. Furthermore, the court's emphasis on transitional justice, including transitional immunity, has led to heated debates regarding its implications for ongoing cases in the UK. As ongoing cases demonstrate that the European Court of Human Rights' continuous evolution tackles the complexities of cross-border violations, it appears that it will continue playing a pivotal role in safeguarding human rights across borders in the years to come.

The "Society and Legislature Act" of 1867, ratified in an atmosphere of heightened British patriotism, heralded the introduction of the voting rights extension to urban, middle-class women. In a recent case, an asylum seeker from Somalia challenged a decision by the UK to refuse their family reunification application. The UK government argued that it had a legitimate interest in maintaining immigration control and had followed a reasonable and transparent decision-making process. The judge ultimately ruled in favour of the applicant, stating that the UK had discriminated against the claimant based on their religion in breach of the Convention. The court emphasized that state interference with family life was permitted only in exceptional circumstances, and in this case, the UK government had not provided sufficient reasons for rejecting the application. The verdict has significant implications for cross-border family reunification and has sparked renewed debate about the regulation of immigration.

The increasing involvement of social media in international relations raises fundamental concerns regarding its impact on human rights. The constant flow of information, often unverified and lacking bias, creates a dynamic environment in which accurate information is hard to discern. This growing reliance on social media, while fostering global connections, can also exacerbate divisions within regions, potentially undermining efforts to promote understanding and respect for fundamental human rights. As the importance of social media in international relations grows, so does its role in affecting individuals and communities. Therefore, countries that prioritize transparency, accountability, and access to accurate information may better navigate the complexities of the virtual world. This intersection of technology with international law creates a complex landscape that requires policymakers to implement measures that balance individual freedoms with collective security, ensuring that benefits of information flow to those in need while preventing exploitation. 1.0: The determination of whether a person is legally entitled to a rehabilitation benefit is a matter of ongoing debate in English law, with different perspectives on the balance of competing rights. Critics argue that the current framework fails to adequately address the needs of individuals who have been incarcerated for lengthy periods, highlighting the need for a more holistic approach to judicial decision-making. In contrast, advocates of the status quo contend that the current standards provide an effective means of balancing individual liberty with the requirements of public safety. Despite these differences, a growing body of scholarship continues to interrogate the relationship between penal rehabilitation and custodial release in human rights jurisprudence, pushing for greater policy coherence and normative clarity in the application of human rights norms.

The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has established a comprehensive framework for the protection of human rights through the European Convention, which enshrines an extensive array of fundamental rights and freedoms. The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) is a tribunal that interprets and enforces human rights in European countries. It is the highest, most permanent, judicial organ of the Council of Europe responsible for deciding cases concerning the protection of human rights as defined by the internationally recognised European Convention on Human Rights and the Federal Constitutional Charter of Europe. The Court consists of 47 judges aged between 35 and 65 years old, elected for a six-year term by the Assembly as part of a rotation system. The judges' languages include English, French and German most of the time when an oral hearing is required by the party that invokes it for the main hearing or judgment, since a minimum of “60 per cent” of the judges have to agree to the decision during this phase in an otherwise identical case. Since the judgments of the ECHR are considered precedents to be applied in all member states, they contribute to the common interpretation of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights within the EU that also includes the protection for minority rights.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently established that the right to a fair trial encompasses both the right to an impartial and independent judiciary and the right to be assisted by an "effective legal aid," as provided for in Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights. In the landmark judgments of _Osman_ and _De Cubenoque_, the ECHR held that the only instances in which an individual may be denied access to public assistance to defend their rights are situations where the public interest fundamentally outweighs the private interests and providing such assistance would cause unreasonable disturbance or prejudice. This restrictive test requires an evaluation during the investigation to determine whether the compelling circumstances are present before a right to effective legal representation can be denied.

While investigating the alleged violations of Article 3 of the European Convention against torture, the Grand Chamber held that the respondent state's failure to investigate disclosed evidence in three separate cases before returning eight prisoners to Kosovo resulted in a breach of the convention. The majority agreed that the applicants would be paid damages and costs. The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the importance of freedom of assembly, a fundamental right enshrined in Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights. On one hand, it allows individuals to express their opinions and beliefs, even in the face of minority perspectives. Conversely, excessive and coordinated gatherings have the potential to disrupt public order, posing a threat to the safety of citizens. As the court has previously ruled, such events must be permitted in accordance with Article 11, as long as they do not pose a danger to public health, order, or morals. By striking a delicate balance between these competing interests, the court has established that freedom to assemble is a necessary check on authoritarian power, while also safeguarding the rights of the community.

The concept of a "legally sound" analysis under Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) has been subject to debate among scholars in recent years. The doctrine of proportionality, set forth by the Grand Chamber in the case of _Handyside_, has been a significant aspect of this discussion. In this case, the ECHR held that a restriction on freedom of expression may be justified if it is proportionate to the aim pursued, necessary in a democratic society and in a manner respecting the dignity and human rights thereof. However, the ECHR has also established that this does not apply where, for example, an accused 'has not been proved to have genuinely held views deserving of respect in a democratic community'; in such a situation the restrictor could instead achieve the required democratic justification by excluding the accused from access to a particular forum or communication channel, rather than removing the content itself.
